does: This paper introduces OPERA, a reinforcement learning approach that aligns language models on open-ended tasks by replacing unreliable external reward models with intrinsic rewards derived from perplexity dynamics. The method quantifies uncertainty reduction at critical reflective states to generate stable learning signals. During cold-start training, OPERA employs data synthesis with carefully designed guiding words to generate diverse reasoning traces and uses perplexity-prioritized rollouts that leverage internal log-probabilities to identify logically consistent reasoning branches. The approach produces a dataset of 20,000 high-quality reasoning trajectories and demonstrates consistent improvements in empirical evaluations.
connects: This work extends prior RL-based approaches that have succeeded in objective reasoning tasks like mathematics and code generation by addressing their failure mode on open-ended tasks. It builds on the recognition that LLM-as-a-judge reward models suffer from stylistic bias and positional inconsistency—problems documented in recent work on reward model limitations. The paper combines insights from uncertainty quantification in neural networks with reinforcement learning, drawing on perplexity as a proxy for model confidence. It also relates to prior work on intrinsic motivation and self-supervision in RL, adapting these principles to the language model setting where internal log-probabilities provide a rich signal for identifying high-quality trajectories without external judges.
enables: This approach opens avenues for applying RL to diverse open-ended generation tasks beyond creative writing, including instruction-following, dialogue, and summarization where external evaluation is unreliable. By demonstrating that intrinsic perplexity signals can substitute for external judges, the work points toward self-improving language models that refine their outputs without human or auxiliary model feedback. The perplexity-prioritized rollout mechanism could inspire broader exploration of using internal model signals for curriculum learning and trajectory selection. Future work could extend this to multi-task learning, investigate the theoretical properties of perplexity-based rewards, and explore whether similar intrinsic signals apply to other model architectures or modalities.
